coredata.dc:title: A novel dual-template molecularly imprinted polymer ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on three-emission carbon quantum dots for accurate naked-eye detection of aflatoxin B1 and zearalenone in vegetable oil
coredata.dc:creator: Chi, Hai
Liu, Guoqin
coredata.dc:description: A molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) fluorescence ratiometric sensor based on three-emission carbon quantum dots (CDs) was successfully developed and used for the visual detection of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) and zearalenone (ZEN). To this end, the optimal ratio of MIP and the reaction site were first calculated by density functional theory. Fluorescence sensors based on B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs were then prepared by the sol-gel method. Finally, the visual detection of AFB1 and ZEN from yellow-green to yellow, red, violet, and finally blue was realized after mixing. Compared to the double-emission ratiometric sensor, the mixed three-emission ratiometric sensor revealed richer fluorescence color evolution and higher sensitivity. Under the optimal conditions, the sensing platform induced fluorescence signals of AFB1 and ZEN within 30 s with dynamic linear ranges of 0.01–100 ng/mL and 0.03–100 ng/mL, as well as detection limits of 3.2 pg/mL and 18 pg/mL, respectively. In sum, insights into the visual recognition and construction of rapid detection platforms of AFB1 and ZEN were provided, promising for future food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Color change
Fluorescence sensors
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Visual detection
Aflatoxin B1
Zearalenone

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of B/G/RCDs/MIPs ratiometric fluorescence sensor for AFB1/ZEN
Selection of molar ratio of template to monomer and molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analysis
Construction and characterization of three-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor for AFB1/ZEN detection
Optimization of identification and detection conditions
Analytical performance of B/G/RCDs/MIPs toward AFB1/ZEN detection
Real sample analysis and performance comparison

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the Key Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province [grant number 2019B020212001], National Natural Science Fund of China [grant numbers U21A20270, 31972002, and 31771895], Ministry of Education Engineering Research Center of Starch & Protein Processing, Guangdong Province Laboratory for Green Processing of Natural Products and Product Safety and the 111 Project.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra and images of BCDs, GCDs, RCDs, B/GCDs/MIPs, B/RCDs/MIPs, and B/G/RCDs/MIPs sensor under a single 365 nm excitation, respectively, and TEM images of (B) BCDs@SiO2 nanoparticles and (C, D) B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs; and (E, F) B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs (remove template); and (G, H) the corresponding B/GCDs/NIPs and B/RCDs/NIPs.
(A)–(C) TEM images of BCDs, GCDs and RCDs; (D)–(F) Diameter distributions of BCDs, GCDs and RCDs.

Fluorescence spectra of MIPs sensor for AFB1/ZEN detection from 0 to 100 ng/mL as indicated by the direction of the arrows, where the sensor contained 7% B/RCDs/MIPs, and (A) 3%, (B) 6%, (C) 9%, (D) 12%, (E) 15%, and (F) 18% B/GCDs/MIPs, respectively. The upper insets give the corresponding fluorescence images under a 365 nm UV lamp.

The fluorescence spectra of B/G/RCDs/MIPs sensor (A, C) and B/G/RCDs/NIPs (B, D) sensors, where (A) and (C) are the fluorescence spectra after AFB1 recognition, with concentrations of 0, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 ng/mL respectively, (B) and (D) are the fluorescence spectra after ZEN recognition, with concentrations of 0, 0.03, 0.3, 3, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 ng/mL respectively. (E) and (F) are the changes in the corresponding fluorescence intensity ratios of AFB1 and ZEN, respectively. The upper illustration is the corresponding fluorescent image, and the addition amount of AFB1/ZEN increases from left to right.

Fluorescence spectra of B/G/RCDs/MIPs sensor (A), B/GCDs/MIPs (B) sensor and B/RCDs/MIPs (C) sensor after recognition of AFB1, the concentrations are 0, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 ng/mL respectively. (D) are the changes in the corresponding fluorescence intensity ratios of AFB1. The upper illustration is the corresponding fluorescent image.
Schematic illustration of (A) Synthesis of three-emission carbon quantum dots; (B) the preparation and detection process of proposedB/G/RCDs/MIPssensor.
Recoveries of AFB1 and ZEN in spiked samples detected by B/G/RCDs/MIPs sensor and validated by UHPLC–MS/MS method.

head.ce:title: A novel dual-template molecularly imprinted polymer ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on three-emission carbon quantum dots for accurate naked-eye detection of aflatoxin B1 and zearalenone in vegetable oil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Engineering, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Province Key Laboratory for Green Processing of Natural Products and Product Safety, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Province Key Laboratory for Green Processing of Natural Products and Product Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Province Key Laboratory for Green Processing of Natural Products and Product Safety, South China University of Technology, Guangzhou 510640, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: No.381 Wushan Road, Tianhe District, Guangzhou 510640, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) fluorescence ratiometric sensor based on three-emission carbon quantum dots (CDs) was successfully developed and used for the visual detection of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) and zearalenone (ZEN). To this end, the optimal ratio of MIP and the reaction site were first calculated by density functional theory. Fluorescence sensors based on B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs were then prepared by the sol-gel method. Finally, the visual detection of AFB1 and ZEN from yellow-green to yellow, red, violet, and finally blue was realized after mixing. Compared to the double-emission ratiometric sensor, the mixed three-emission ratiometric sensor revealed richer fluorescence color evolution and higher sensitivity. Under the optimal conditions, the sensing platform induced fluorescence signals of AFB1 and ZEN within 30 s with dynamic linear ranges of 0.01–100 ng/mL and 0.03–100 ng/mL, as well as detection limits of 3.2 pg/mL and 18 pg/mL, respectively. In sum, insights into the visual recognition and construction of rapid detection platforms of AFB1 and ZEN were provided, promising for future food analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mycotoxins are secondary metabolites produced during the growth and reproduction of fungi [1]. Mycotoxins can be absorbed by organisms through the food chain, posing a serious threat to the breeding industry and human health [2]. Examples of mycotoxins include aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) [3] and zearalenone (ZEN) [4] with the most serious economic impact. Therefore, developing effective methods for the analysis and quantification of trace AFB1 and ZEN residues in food samples is of great importance to environmental protection and public health [5]. Numerous methods have so far been developed for the analysis of AFB1 and ZEN, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6], ultra-high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (UHPLC–MS) [7], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays [8], as well as fluorescence, electrochemical, colorimetric, and other detection technologies. Among these, novel sensing technologies based on fluorescence have attracted more attention in food safety and environmental monitoring owing to their simplicity, sensitivity, rapid analysis, and easy visualization [9].

The key to high-performance fluorescent sensors relies on the specificity of target substances. As an antibody-like artificial material with predetermined structure and activity [10–12], molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with excellent selectivity, high chemical properties, and good physical stability [13] have attracted increasing attention in the field of the environment [14], biology [15], and food [16]. The performance of fluorescent sensors also depends on fluorescent materials. The combination of MIP with fluorescent materials characterized by specific recognition ability has been used as an ideal strategy to build sensing platforms in the past decade. Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have outstanding advantages in terms of excellent stability [17], high water solubility [18], and biocompatibility [19], making them potential substitutes for semiconductor quantum dots [20]. For example, Hu et al. encapsulated magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Mg, N-CDs) in molecularly imprinted polymers (Mg, N-CDs@MIP) for the first time to achieve specific recognition and ultra-sensitive detection of tetracycline in animal-derived foods [21], where (Mg, N-CDs) were employed as signal labels to significantly improve the sensitivity of fluorescence sensors. Wang et al. reported a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence sensor based on a magnetic covalent organic framework supported carbon point imprinted molecular-imprinted complex with a detection limit toward bisphenol A in food and boxed beverages as low as 12 μg/L [22]. However, most studies have been focused on fluorescent CDs excited by a single wavelength, with sensing applications often disturbed by the nature of the environment and concentration of the media, resulting in low sensitivity and poor visibility [23]. Therefore, more research is redirected toward the visualization of CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors with built-in corrections to avoid environmental influences [24]. For example, Wang et al. introduced blue and red double emission CDs in ZIF-8 [25]. The strong luminescence and high stability of the composite resulted in efficient detection of Pb2+. In another study, Jalili et al. proposed a dual-CDs-based fluorescence sensor for the determination of penicillin G in food by realizing continuous fluorescence changes from yellow to blue that can be seen by the naked eye [26]. However, despite the good sensitivity and detection ability of reported dual-CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors, they still suffer from difficulties in capturing changes under bright fluorescence. Therefore, expanding the color range and studying the CDs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors are important tasks for developing advanced sensors.

Note that the surface molecularly imprinted cavity is located on the polymer surface. Compared to traditional preparation method of fluorescent MIPs, the higher mass transfer rate and more binding sites of the above method make it widely applicable for the preparation of fluorescent MIPs [27,28]. Among them, the sol gel method is the most commonly used to prepare fluorescent MIPs [29,30]. Since imprinting materials with high biocompatibility obtained by sol-gel the method possess low porosity and high specific surface area, they can easily be approached by target molecules for a possible reaction. In addition, the preparation of traditional MIPs suffers from high costs and elevated toxicity of template molecules, leading to limited use of MIPs [31]. Alternatively, virtual-template molecules are used as a replacement to form recognition sites and produce MIPs with higher affinity and lower mass transfer resistance [32]. The traditional single-molecule MIPs fluorescence sensors can easily be utilized but multi-molecule imprinting is still challenging. Therefore, developing MIPs fluorescence sensors for dual/multi-molecule recognition that can effectively save preparation time, cost, and labor is highly desirable for expanding the diversity of fluorescence sensing in food detection.

To the best of our knowledge, the use of dual-template molecularly imprinted polymer ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on three-emission CDs has not been yet reported for the detection of AFB1 or ZEN. Therefore, a simple and green strategy was developed in this study to incorporate blue CDs (BCDs) into SiO2 as an internal reference. Red CDs (RCDs) and green CDs (GCDs)-embedded molecularly imprinted layers were then constructed on BCDs@SiO2 surface by the sol-gel method. Specifically, blue, green, and red CDs with a single wavelength were prepared by the hydrothermal reaction using citric acid and ethylenediamine in different solvents. The binding energy and molecular electrostatic potential of virtual template molecules (5,7-dimethoxycoumarin and warfarin) with functional monomer complexes were then calculated by the density functional theory (DFT). Guided by theoretical calculations, novel ratiometric fluorescence sensors were fabricated based on the deposition of B/RCDs/MIPs and B/GCDs/MIPs on BCDs@SiO2 surface. The resulting ratiometric fluorescence sensors exhibited a color change from yellow-green to yellow, red, violet, and blue during visual detection of trace AFB1 or ZEN at appropriate proportions. Compared to B/RCDs/MIPs and B/GCDs/MIPs, the mixed B/G/RCDs/MIPs showed also better analytical performance. The determination of these species in spiked vegetable oil samples further demonstrated the reliable and sensitive features of B/G/RCDs/MIPs-based fluorescence sensing platforms toward the detection of trace amounts of AFB1 or ZEN.

Citric acid (CA), Neutral red (NR), Urea (CH4N2O), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4), 5,7-Dimethoxycoumarin (C11H10O4), warfarin (C19H16O4), zearalenone (ZEN), aflatoxin G1 (AFG1), aflatoxin G2 (AFG2), aflatoxin B2 (AFB2), and fumonisin B1 (FB1) were all purchased from Macklin Co. Ltd. Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), ethylenediamine (EDA), acetone (CH3COCH3), deoxynivalenol (DON), and ochratoxin A (OTA) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. Ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O) was received from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Ethanol (C2H5OH) and methanol (CH3OH) were provided by Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemical Co. Ltd. All reagents were of analytical grade purity and used as received without further purification. Naturally-contaminated vegetable oil samples were obtained from local supermarkets.

The fluorescence spectra were collected on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Tianmei, China, Scientific Instrument Co. Ltd) at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. UV–Vis spectrometry was performed on Cary 60 instrument (Agilent Technologies). The morphologies of the samples were viewed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Merlin, Zeiss) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2100F). The FTIR spectra were collected on a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Nicolet IS50-Nicolet Continuum, Thermo Fisher Scientific).

The structures of 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin, warfarin, and APTES at different proportions were constructed, and their geometries were optimized through the self-consistent charge density-functional tight-binding (SCC-DFTB) [33] method by dispersion correction using DFTB + program [34]. A smart algorithm with force convergence tolerance of 0.05 kcal/mol/Å was used with SCC tolerance set to 1.0×10−5 electrons [35]. Based on the optimized structure, single-point calculations were carried out at the M06-2X/6–311 + g(d,p) level of theory corrected with Grimme’s dispersion (D3) [36] using the Gaussian 09 software package [37].

BCDs [38], GCDs [39], and RCDs [40] were synthesized by procedures reported in the literature with appropriate modifications. Briefly, 1.1 g CA and 0.3 mL EDA were dissolved in 20 mL water. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After natural cooling, the pellets were collected by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 20 min. The precipitate was then washed several times with ethanol and water, and BCDs were collected by vacuum drying. For the synthesis of GCDs (CA: 2 g, Urea: 4 g), the reaction solvent was changed to ethanol, and the heating temperature was set to 160 ℃. For RCDs preparation, 5 g CA and 6.2 mg NR were used as precursors at a heating temperature set to 180 ℃. The obtained reaction products were centrifuged and the resulting supernatant was further purified at pore diameter of 0.22 μM followed by drying under vacuum to yield GCDs and RCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of B/G/RCDs/MIPs ratiometric fluorescence sensor for AFB1/ZEN
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescence sensor B/GCDs/MIPs for AFB1/ZEN detection was prepared by a one-pot sol–gel method [41]. Briefly, 0.6 mL BCDs were added to a 50 mL ethanol solution. Next, 0.4 mL NH3·H2O and 50 µL TEOS were sequentially injected and stirred for 12 h followed by the addition of 0.8 mL GCDs, 52 μL APTES, 46 mg warfarin, and 10 mg 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin. After stirring for 1 h, 0.5 mL NH3·H2O and 0.15 mL TEOS were added followed by stirring for 12 h in dark. The resulting mixture was finally centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 20 min and the precipitate was repeatedly eluted with methanol to remove template molecules, then dispersed in 10 mL PBS buffer (pH 8.0) for further experiments. For comparison, non-imprinted B/GCDs material (B/GCDs/NIPs) was prepared as a control sample using the same synthetic method but without the addition of AFB1 and ZEN. Likewise, B/RCDs/MIPs and corresponding B/RCDs/NIPs were prepared according to B/GCDs/MIPs and B/GCDs/NIPs synthesis methods but using RCDs instead of GCDs. For better AFB1/ZEN visualization, B/G/RCDs/MIPs were incubated for 10 min by thoroughly dispersing B/RCDs/MIPs (7%, v/v) and B/GCDs/MIPs (12%, v/v) in PBS buffer containing AFB1/ZEN. The fluorescence spectra were then recorded at a single excitation wavelength of 365 nm with a slit width of 5 nm. For better reproducibility, the essays were performed at room temperature with 3 replicates for each test value.

Corn and peanut oil samples were purchased from a local market (Guangzhou, China) and used to quantify AFB1 and ZEN in vegetable oil. The extraction method was based on the method reported by Li et al. with slight modifications [42]. Briefly, 5 g oil samples were weighted, and different concentrations of AFB1 and ZEN standard solutions were transferred to oil samples followed by mixing with 25 mL methanol aqueous solution (7:3, v/v) and vortexing under oscillation for 30 min. Afterward, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, and the collected supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and evaporated until dry. Finally, a PBS buffer (pH 8.0) was used to re-dissolve the collected residue, and the extracted solution was incubated and analyzed with prepared B/G/RCDs/MIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of molar ratio of template to monomer and molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The binding sites, number, and selectivity of MIPs were determined by analyzing the binding energy and number of hydrogen bonds involved during the polymerization process. The results indicated different stabilities of MIPs formed by various detection substances and functional monomers. However, fewer monomers produced only fewer binding sites, while numerous monomers induced nonspecific binding sites. Therefore, the reasonable design and optimization of MIPs may not only provide an in-deep understanding of the interaction mechanism between the template molecules and functional monomers but also save time and reduce errors during the experimental process. In this work, the structures of 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin, warfarin, and monomer APTES were all optimized (Fig. S1 of the Supporting Information). Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analysis was carried out to identify the interaction sites of 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin, warfarin, and APTES (Fig. S2). The O atoms of 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin and warfarin possessed negative MEP, while the H atoms in the NH2 group of APTES had positive MEP. Thus, the hydrogen-bonding interaction sites might have occurred between the oxygen atoms of 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin or warfarin and the hydrogen atom in the NH2 group of APTES. Also, the π – π stacking interaction between the aromatic rings of 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin and warfarin took place. Based on these analyses, the interaction models were constructed, with interaction between 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin and warfarin based on the π – π stacking interaction, while those between APTES and 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin (or warfarin) were based on the hydrogen-bonding interaction. To obtain the optimal ratio, the ratios of 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin, warfarin, and APTES with 1:1:5, 1:2:5, 1:3:5, 2:1:5, and 3:1:5 were calculated, and their optimized structures were shown in Fig. S3. The results revealed that a ratio of 1:3:5 would induce hydrogen bonding between the monomer and template, as well as weak intermolecular interaction between the templates through π – π stacking [43], resulting in the lowest binding energy (Table S1) and the most stable complex molecules.

The possible molecular recognition information between the interaction of the monomer and template was collected by theoretically simulating possible binding modes between the monomer and template. The most stable ratio (1:3:5) was selected as the research case and the information on possible interaction sites between 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin, warfarin, and APTES was obtained by MEP calculations. The O atoms of 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin and warfarin were based on negative MEP, while the H atoms in the NH2 group of APTES had positive MEP. Thus, the hydrogen-bonding interaction sites occurred between the oxygen atoms of 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin or warfarin and the hydrogen atom in the NH2 group of APTES. Also, the π-π stacking interaction between the aromatic rings of 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin and warfarin took place. Based on these analyses, the interaction models were constructed, in which the interaction between 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin and warfarin was based on the π-π stacking interaction while that between APTES and 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin (or warfarin) was based on the hydrogen-bonding interaction. In the MEP diagram (Fig. S4), blue represented the positive electrostatic potential region linked to the electron-deficient region, while yellow corresponded to the negative electrostatic potential region associated with the electron-rich region. In Fig. S4, the O atom represented a negative electrostatic potential region in all organic molecules, while the H atom referred to a positive electrostatic potential region in all organic molecules. Therefore, the adsorbed active sites were mainly made of the oxygen atom of the carbonyl group and the hydrogen atom of the amino group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and characterization of three-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor for AFB1/ZEN detection
ce:section.ce:para: The construction process of the three-emission B/G/RCDs/MIPs ratiometric fluorescence sensor for AFB1/ZEN trace detection was shown in Scheme 1 . In this process, the three-emission CDs in Scheme 1(A) were first prepared by the hydrothermal reaction in different solvents. BCDs-embedded silica nanoparticles (BCDs@SiO2) were then obtained by the Stober method [44]. The coated silica shell of BCDs prevented their direct contact with external template molecules, providing a reliable reference signal for ratiometric detection. Next, 5,7-dimethoxycoumarin and warfarin were utilized as virtual templates, APTES as functional monomer, and TEOS as a cross-linking agent to form AFB1/ZEN-imprinted silica shells on BCDs@SiO2 surface in presence of GCDs or RCDs. Finally, highly specific AFB1/ZEN imprinted B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs ratiometric sensors were obtained after the removal of the template. As shown in Fig. 1 (A), the resulting ratiometric fluorescence sensor after appropriate proportion mixing showed multimodal emissions at 456, 517, and 619 nm at 365 nm excitation wavelength. The contact between the recognition site in B/G/RCDs/MIPs with AFB1/ZEN resulted in multiple color changes of “on” blue fluorescence, while “off” green and red fluorescence appeared. This revealed a color change from yellow-green to yellow, red, violet, and blue, allowing an accurate determination of AFB1/ZEN with the naked eye (Scheme 1 (B)).

The morphologies of BCDs, GCDs, RCDs, B/GCDs/MIPs, and B/RCDs/MIPs, as well as their corresponding NIPs were studied by TEM and SEM techniques. In Fig. 2 , uniform and monodisperse BCDs, GCDs, and RCDs were visible, with average particle sizes estimated to 2.44, 3.09, and 4.39 nm, respectively. In Fig. 1(B) and Fig. S5(A), BCDs@SiO2 nanoparticles looked spherical and evenly dispersed. Meanwhile, B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs, as well as their corresponding NIPs showed similar spherical morphologies (Fig. 1), while SEM images in Fig. S5 displayed obviously rough and irregular surfaces. However, the surfaces of B/GCDs/MIPs (Fig. 1(E)) and B/RCDs/MIPs (Fig. 1(F)) after removing the template showed certain roughness, indicating the successful preparation of MIPs to some extent. In addition, the particle diameter after coating with MIPs significantly increased when compared to BCDs@SiO2, meaning successfully prepared MIPs on BCDs@SiO2.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to further study BCDs@SiO2, B/GCDs/MIPs, and B/RCDs/MIPs along with their NIPs, as well as B/GCD/MIPs and B/RCD/MIPs with the analyte. In Fig. S6, the wide absorption band at 3455 cm−1 could be attributed to O-H stretching vibration of SiO2, while that at 1095 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of Si-O-Si. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks at 460 and 778 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration of Si-O. Compared to BCDs@SiO2, the absorption peaks around 1550 cm−1 in MIPs and NIPs could be attributed to the N-H stretching vibration, indicating the presence of aminopropyl group of APTES. After binding B/GCD/MIPs and B/RCD/MIPs with the analyte, two peaks appeared at 1446 and 1399 cm−1, indicating surface analyte bounded to APTES through hydrogen bonding. Meanwhile, the main bands of MIPs and NIPs spectra displayed similar intensities and frequencies, revealing the successful preparation of MIPs and NIPs.

The elemental compositions of B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs were studied by XPS. As shown in Fig. S7, B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs depicted four distinct peaks, corresponding to C1s, O1s, Si2p, and N1s signals, respectively. Thus, B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs were composed of carbon, oxygen, silicon, and nitrogen, consistent with FT-IR results and further confirming Si atoms effectively covering the surfaces of CDs. The presence of all these peaks indicated successfully generated B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs. Overall, all the above characterization results confirmed successfully introduced and coated MIPs/NIPs on BCDs@SiO2 surfaces.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of identification and detection conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain highly sensitive analytical results, the identification conditions were optimized by focusing mainly on three key parameters: i) optimal ratio of B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs mixing, ii) pH of the solvent, and iii) optimal response time of B/G/RCDs/MIPs.

The volume fractions of B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs would determine the visualization effect of B/G/RCDs/MIPs. Therefore, the color change of B/G/RCDs/MIPs was tested by adding AFB1 (0–100 ng/mL). To this end, the volume fraction of B/RCDs/MIPs was first fixed at 7% (v/v), while that of B/GCDs/MIPs was controlled from 3% to 18% (v/v). As shown in Fig. 3 , very low amounts of B/GCDs/MIPs did not induce yellow fluorescence, while very high B/GCDs/MIPs covered red fluorescence. The same study was conducted on ZEN and the data revealed 12% (v/v) B/GCDs/MIPs to provide the most variable fluorescence. At a volume fraction of 12%, a color transition was noticed from yellow-green to yellow, red, violet, and blue, realizing a visual analysis with the naked eye.

The pH value may also affect the fluorescence intensity and template recombination efficiency of B/G/RCDs/MIPs. Therefore, the influence of the fluorescence intensity ratio of AFB1/ZEN (50 ng/mL) in PBS buffer was investigated at pH values of 5–10. In Fig. S8(A and B), the fluorescence intensity ratio of B/G/RCDs/MIPs displayed a maximum value at pH 8.0 for both AFB1 and ZEN owing to the ionization of SiO2 in an alkaline environment to damage the structure of MIPs. Therefore, the optimal pH of the resorbed template molecule was determined as pH 8.

The optimal adsorption time of the sensor may also impact the detection properties, thereby being studied by selecting different reaction time response ratios between AFB1/ZEN and B/G/RCDs/MIPs. As shown in Fig. S8(C and D), the addition of 50 ng/mL AFB1/ZEN led to an upward trend fluorescence intensity ratio from 0 to 10 min. However, no significant changes were noticed after 10 min. Therefore, 10 min was selected as the optimal response time for B/G/RCDs/MIPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of B/G/RCDs/MIPs toward AFB1/ZEN detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity ratios of B/G/RCDs/MIPs and B/G/RCDs/NIPs at different concentrations of AFB1 or ZEN were investigated to evaluate the effect of B/G/RCDs/MIPs sensors on the detection of AFB1/ZEN under the optimal conditions. Before identifying AFB1 or Zen (Fig. 4 ), B/G/RCDs/MIPs showed three emission peaks at 456, 517, and 619 nm at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. After the addition of AFB1 or ZEN, the fluorescence intensities of GCDs (517 nm) and RCDs (619 nm) became gradually quenched, while that of BCDs (456 nm) remained relatively constant. The slight change in intensities of the two peaks resulted in a distinguishable and rich color change (yellow-green to yellow, red, violet, and blue) due to the simultaneous reduction in fluorescence intensity. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity and color change of the B/G/RCDs/NIPs sensor were very slight when compared to B/G/RCDs/MIPs (Fig. 4(B and D)), owing to the lack of sites for specific recognition of AFB1 or ZEN. In Fig. 4(E and F), the B/G/RCDs/MIPs sensor showed good linear relationships for the detection of AFB1 and ZEN in the ranges of 0.01–100 ng/mL and 0.03–100 ng/mL, with R2 values of 0.9931 and 0.9964, respectively. According to 3SD/slope, the detection limits were recorded as 3.2 pg/mL and 18 pg/mL (LOD), respectively.

The values of the imprinting factor (IF) of the linear slope ratios of B/G/RCDs/MIPs and B/G/RCDs/NIPs were further calculated to illustrate the specificity of B/G/RCDs/MIPs. The results showed values as high as 21.89 and 21.95, respectively. Higher IF indicated B/G/RCDs/MIPs with elevated specificity toward the detection of AFB1/ZEN.

The quenching mechanism of B/G/RCDs/MIPs on AFB1/ZEN in Fig. S9 revealed that the absorption spectrum of AFB1/ZEN did not overlap with the excitation or emission spectrum of the polymer, demonstrating quenching mechanism not based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer. AFB1/ZEN could form complexes with specific recognition sites B/G/RCDs/MIPs through hydrogen bonding. The above results indicated that the fluorescence quenching mechanism may be due to the synergistic effect of electron transfer. The fluorescence of B/G/RCDs/MIPs was quenched owing to the non-radiative deactivation of GCDs and RCDs during electron transfer and interaction between adsorption and binding imprinting layers, resulting in fluorescence quenching. Further study of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of B/G/RCDs/MIPs by AFB1/ZEN was also carried out by Stern-Volmer equation.
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K S V
[ Q ]
where F0 and F are the strengths of B/G/RCDs/MIPs with and without AFB1/ZEN treatment, respectively. Ksv represents the Stern-Volmer constant, and [Q] is the concentration of the quenching agent.

Based on this equation, Ksv was obtained at three temperatures of 298, 308, and 318 K (Table S2). As the temperature rose, Ksv value decreased, suggesting a quenching static property. The quenching mechanism was also verified by fluorescence lifetime measurements (Fig. S10). The fluorescence lifetime of B/G/RCDs/MIPs was estimated to 4.10 ns before and 3.96 ns after the addition of the analyte. This also indirectly excluded fluorescence resonance energy transfer, further confirming fluorescence quenching mechanism based on static quenching. The values of binding constant Kb of AFB1/ZEN and B/G/RCDs/MIPs were also measured according to the following equations:
(2)
L o g
F 0
- F
F
= l o g
K b
+ n l o g
[ Q ]
(3)
Ln
K b
= -
Δ H
RT
+
Δ S
R
(4)
Δ G = Δ H - T Δ S

The values of each parameter of interaction between B/G/RCDs/MIPs and AFB1/ZEN were recorded and the results were listed in Table S2. Overall, the binding reaction was reversible and driven by hydrogen bonding.

To further prove the good sensitivity and visualization of the B/G/RCDs/MIPs sensor, AFB1 (0–100 ng/mL) was added to B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs to test their color changes and fluorescence intensities, respectively. In Fig. 5 B, B/GCDs/MIPs revealed only a color change from green to light, blue, and then dark blue, while B/RCDs/MIPs displayed a color change from red to violet and then blue (Fig. 5C). Besides, the fluorescence intensity ratio changes of both ratiometric sensors were significantly lower than those of B/G/RCDs/MIPs, suggesting lower sensitivities (Fig. 5D). Therefore, the mixed B/G/RCDs/MIPs were advantageous in term of variation in fluorescence intensity ratio or color.

A key issue in the development of fluorescent sensors relies on selectivity. Hence, structural analogs of AFB1 (AFG1, AFG2, AFB2), and ZEN (FB1, DON, OTA) were tested to study the selectivity of B/G/RCDs/MIPs. As shown in Fig. S11(A – D), the change in the fluorescence intensity ratio of the B/G/RCDs/MIPs sensor was significant in the presence of AFB1/ZEN, while those of chemical structure analogs looked significantly reduced. B/G/RCDs/NIPs displayed similar and close fluorescence intensity ratio changes toward AFB1/ZEN and its analogs. Thus, the polymerization layer on B/G/RCDs/NIPs surface did not possess the specific recognition site of AFB1/ZEN.

The fluorescence stability of B/G/RCDs/MIPs was also tested by irradiating with artificial UV light at 365 nm and 10 min intervals. As shown in Fig. S11E, the fluorescence intensity ratio after 60 min irradiation decreased by less than 2.9%. Furthermore, B/G/RCDs/MIPs sensor retained 95.4% of its original response after three weeks of storage at 4 °C (Fig. S11F). Therefore, the highly cross-linked silica imprinted layer led to excellent selectivity, stability, and acceptable storage stability for B/G/RCDs/MIPs ratiometric sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis and performance comparison
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application in food, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on dual-template B/G/RCDs/MIPs for AFB1 or ZEN detection was subjected to recovery experiments in corn and peanut oil samples. As listed in Table 1 , the addition of three concentration levels of AFB1 and ZEN (5, 10, and 50 ng/mL) to oil samples resulted in recoveries of 96.3% to 103.7% and 99.1% to 102.6%, respectively, with relative deviations of less than 5.0%. Similar results were also obtained by UHPLC-MS/MS (specific method shown in supplementary data), verifying the reliability of B/G/RCDs/MIPs applied to trace AFB1 or ZEN in actual samples.

The analytical performance of the prepared B/G/RCDs/MIPs was compared to those of reported molecular imprinting methods for the determination of AFB1 and ZEN. As depicted in Table S3, the developed B/G/RCDs/MIPs sensor compared well or better to reported values in terms of linear range and detection limit. In addition, the increase in AFB1 or ZEN resulted in green and red emission quenching, allowing variation in the fluorescence color from yellow-green to yellow, red, violet, and finally to blue for easy visual detection. More importantly, B/G/RCDs/MIPs sensors based on CDs fluorescent probes would be more economical and environmentally friendly, and can specifically recognize AFB1 and ZEN while providing lower LOD and better linear range. Therefore, B/G/RCDs/MIPs-based fluorescence sensors would be suitable for individual quantitative detection of AFB1 or ZEN in vegetable oil samples.

A novel ratio fluorescence sensor was successfully constructed by optimizing the proportion of B/GCDs/MIPs and B/RCDs/MIPs with double templates. The resulting sensors were used for the highly sensitive, selective, and visual detection of AFB1 or ZEN in real samples. The optimal ratio between double template molecule and functional monomer was simulated by quantum chemistry to provide theoretical guidance for the design of MIPs.

The B/G/RCDs/MIPs fluorescence sensor turned to green and red fluorescence when recognizing AFB1 or ZEN, thereby producing a color transition from yellow-green to yellow, red, violet, and blue. In addition, the developed sensor was environmentally friendly and green. Under optimized experimental conditions, AFB1 and ZEN exhibited satisfactory linearity at concentration ranges of 0.01–100 ng/mL and 0.03–100 ng/mL, with LOD values of 3.2 pg/mL and 18 pg/mL, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed B/G/RCDs/MIPs fluorescence sensor successfully detected AFB1 and ZEN in vegetable oil samples. Overall, the proposed sensor was efficient, sensitive, and accurate for the visual detection of AFB1 or ZEN. However, other analytes would require recalculation and customization to achieve qualitative and quantitative analysis in food samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by the Key Research and Development Program of Guangdong Province [grant number 2019B020212001], National Natural Science Fund of China [grant numbers U21A20270, 31972002, and 31771895], Ministry of Education Engineering Research Center of Starch & Protein Processing, Guangdong Province Laboratory for Green Processing of Natural Products and Product Safety and the 111 Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108961.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesized carbon dots embedded in silica molecularly imprinted polymers, characterization and application as a rapid and selective fluorimetric sensor for determination of thiabendazole in juices
Food Chemistry
